rawHead:
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Mathematics and cryptography</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- breadcrumb-head-auto-begin -->

rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 Modular arithmetic 2 RSA 3 Discrete log 4 Elliptic curves 5 Lattice / PQC 6 Zero-knowledge

Mathematics and cryptography

A guided tour from (\mathbb{Z}/n)^\times through RSA, Diffie–Hellman, elliptic curves, and the post-quantum lattice frontier — with a coda on zero-knowledge proofs.

blocks.html:
1. Modular arithmetic and Euler's theorem

Public-key cryptography lives inside the multiplicative group $(\mathbb{Z}/n)^\times$ — the units modulo $n$, i.e. integers coprime to $n$ under multiplication. Its order is Euler's totient $$\phi(n) = \#\{a : 1\le a\le n,\ \gcd(a,n)=1\}.$$ For a prime power, $\phi(p^k) = p^{k-1}(p-1)$, and $\phi$ is multiplicative on coprime arguments. The key identity, the Fermat–Euler theorem, falls out of Lagrange's theorem applied to the unit group:

$$\gcd(a,n)=1 \implies a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n.$$

When $n=p$ is prime, $(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$ is cyclic of order $p-1$, and a generator is called a primitive root. Powers of a primitive root enumerate every nonzero residue exactly once — this is the algebraic substrate every classical cryptosystem will rent.
W1 · Euler totient and unit groupchange $n$ to see $(\mathbb{Z}/n)^\times$
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const out = $("#w-tot-out"), svg = $("#w-tot-svg");
  function go(){
    const n = parseInt($("#w-tot-n").value, 10);
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    if(!Number.isFinite(n) || n < 2 || n > 60){
      out.textContent = "need 2 ≤ n ≤ 60";
      return;
    }
    const units = [];
    for(let a=1; a<n; a++){ if(gcdInt(a,n)===1) units.push(a); }
    const phi = units.length;
    out.textContent =
      `n = ${n}\n` +
      `φ(n) = ${phi}    [#units in (Z/${n})×]\n` +
      `units = {${units.join(", ")}}`;
    // grid: cols * rows = n, draw n cells
    const W = 720, H = 220, pad = 16;
    const cols = Math.min(15, n);
    const rows = Math.ceil(n / cols);
    const cw = (W - 2*pad) / cols;
    const ch = Math.min(28, (H - 2*pad - 30) / rows);
    for(let a=0; a<n; a++){
      const r = Math.floor(a/cols), c = a%cols;
      const x = pad + c*cw, y = pad + r*ch;
      const isUnit = (a>0) && gcdInt(a,n)===1;
      const isZero = (a===0);
      const fill = isUnit ? "var(--yellow)" : (isZero ? "var(--panel2)" : "var(--panel)");
      const stroke = isUnit ? "var(--yellow)" : "var(--line)";
      svg.appendChild(SVG("rect", {x: x+1, y: y+1, width: cw-2, height: ch-2,
        fill, stroke, "stroke-width": 1, opacity: isUnit ? 0.85 : 1, rx: 3}));
      const t = SVG("text", {x: x + cw/2, y: y + ch/2 + 4, "text-anchor": "middle",
        "font-size": 12, fill: isUnit ? "#111" : "var(--ink)"});
      t.textContent = a;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    const legend = SVG("text", {x: pad, y: H - 14, "font-size": 12, fill: "var(--mute)"});
    legend.textContent = `yellow = unit, count = φ(${n}) = ${phi}`;
    svg.appendChild(legend);
  }
  $("#w-tot-go").addEventListener("click", go);
  $("#w-tot-n").addEventListener("change", go);
  go();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Used in
Mathematics and cryptography · Discrete logarithm and Diffie–Hellman
Mathematics and cryptography · RSA

2. RSA

Rivest–Shamir–Adleman (1977) is the original public-key cryptosystem. Key generation: pick two large primes $p, q$ and set $$n = pq, \qquad \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1).$$ Choose a public exponent $e$ with $\gcd(e,\phi(n))=1$ (commonly $e=65537$), and compute the private exponent $$d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}.$$ Encryption raises a message $m$ to the public exponent, decryption raises ciphertext to the private:

$$c \equiv m^e \pmod n, \qquad m \equiv c^d \pmod n.$$

Why does this work? By construction $ed = 1 + k\phi(n)$ for some integer $k$, so for any $m$ coprime to $n$ Fermat–Euler gives

$$c^d = m^{ed} = m^{1+k\phi(n)} = m \cdot (m^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv m \cdot 1^k = m \pmod n.$$

(A short CRT argument extends correctness to all residues, even when $\gcd(m,n)>1$.)

Security rests on the conjectured hardness of factoring $n$. If you can factor, you recover $\phi(n)$ and hence $d$. The converse is also true: knowing $d$ lets you (probabilistically) factor $n$, so the private key and the factorization are essentially the same secret.

RSA assumption. Strictly, RSA security needs not just factoring-hardness but the RSA problem — given $(n,e,c)$, find $m$ with $m^e\equiv c$. No separation from factoring is known, but textbook RSA is broken in practice (deterministic, malleable, leaky), and real deployments use OAEP or PSS padding.
W2 · Toy RSAset primes & encrypt a number
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const out = $("#w-rsa-out"), svg = $("#w-rsa-svg");
  function go(){
    const p = parseInt($("#w-rsa-p").value, 10);
    const q = parseInt($("#w-rsa-q").value, 10);
    const e = parseInt($("#w-rsa-e").value, 10);
    const m = parseInt($("#w-rsa-m").value, 10);
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    if(!isPrimeSmall(p) || !isPrimeSmall(q) || p===q){
      out.textContent = "need distinct primes p, q";
      return;
    }
    const n = p*q;
    const phi = (p-1)*(q-1);
    if(gcdInt(e, phi) !== 1){
      out.textContent = `e=${e} is not coprime to φ(n)=${phi}`;
      return;
    }
    if(!Number.isFinite(m) || m < 0 || m >= n){
      out.textContent = `need 0 ≤ m < n = ${n}`;
      return;
    }
    const d = modInv(e, phi);
    const c = modPow(m, e, n);
    const mBack = modPow(c, d, n);
    out.textContent =
      `n = p*q = ${p}*${q} = ${n}\n` +
      `φ(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = ${phi}\n` +
      `public  (n, e) = (${n}, ${e})\n` +
      `private  d = e^-1 mod φ(n) = ${d}\n` +
      `encrypt: c = m^e mod n = ${m}^${e} mod ${n} = ${c}\n` +
      `decrypt: m = c^d mod n = ${c}^${d} mod ${n} = ${mBack}    ${(mBack===m)?"✓":"✗"}`;
    // arrow diagram m -> c -> m
    drawNode(svg,  90, 70, "m", {r: 26, stroke: "var(--green)"});
    drawNode(svg, 360, 70, "c", {r: 26, stroke: "var(--pink)"});
    drawNode(svg, 630, 70, "m", {r: 26, stroke: "var(--green)"});
    drawArrow(svg, [115, 70], [335, 70], {color: "var(--yellow)", label: `()^${e} mod ${n}`, markerId: "arr-rsa-enc"});
    drawArrow(svg, [385, 70], [605, 70], {color: "var(--cyan)", label: `()^${d} mod ${n}`, markerId: "arr-rsa-dec"});
    const t = SVG("text", {x: 360, y: 130, "text-anchor": "middle", "font-size": 12, fill: "var(--mute)"});
    t.textContent = `m=${m}    c=${c}    decrypted=${mBack}`;
    svg.appendChild(t);
  }
  $("#w-rsa-go").addEventListener("click", go);
  ["#w-rsa-p","#w-rsa-q","#w-rsa-e","#w-rsa-m"].forEach(s => $(s).addEventListener("change", go));
  go();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
3. Discrete logarithm and Diffie–Hellman

Fix a prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$ of $(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$. Every $h \in (\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$ has a unique exponent $$x \in \{0,1,\dots,p-2\} \quad\text{with}\quad g^x \equiv h \pmod p.$$ The discrete logarithm $\log_g h$ is that exponent. Computing it is conjecturally hard; verifying $g^x=h$ is one fast exponentiation. This one-way function is the engine of Diffie–Hellman key exchange (1976):

  1. Public parameters: a prime $p$ and primitive root $g$.
  2. Alice picks secret $a$, sends $A = g^a \bmod p$.
  3. Bob picks secret $b$, sends $B = g^b \bmod p$.
  4. Both compute $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$: Alice as $B^a$, Bob as $A^b$.

The eavesdropper sees $g, p, g^a, g^b$ and must compute $g^{ab}$ — the computational Diffie–Hellman problem. The best known classical algorithm is index calculus, sub-exponential of complexity $L_p[1/3,c]$, which is why DH key sizes are pushed to $\ge 3072$ bits.

W3 · Diffie–Hellman key exchangechange secrets to see the shared key
6 15
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const out = $("#w-dh-out"), svg = $("#w-dh-svg");
  const aVal = $("#w-dh-a-val"), bVal = $("#w-dh-b-val");
  function go(){
    const p = parseInt($("#w-dh-p").value, 10);
    const g = parseInt($("#w-dh-g").value, 10);
    const a = parseInt($("#w-dh-a").value, 10);
    const b = parseInt($("#w-dh-b").value, 10);
    aVal.textContent = a; bVal.textContent = b;
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    if(!isPrimeSmall(p) || g <= 1 || g >= p){
      out.textContent = "need a small prime p and 2 ≤ g < p";
      return;
    }
    const A = modPow(g, a, p);
    const B = modPow(g, b, p);
    const KAlice = modPow(B, a, p);
    const KBob   = modPow(A, b, p);
    out.textContent =
      `public:  p = ${p},  g = ${g}\n` +
      `Alice:  secret a=${a},  sends A = g^a = ${A}\n` +
      `Bob:    secret b=${b},  sends B = g^b = ${B}\n` +
      `Alice computes K = B^a mod p = ${KAlice}\n` +
      `Bob   computes K = A^b mod p = ${KBob}\n` +
      `✓ shared key K = g^(ab) = ${KAlice}    ${(KAlice===KBob)?"✓":"✗"}`;
    // diagram
    drawNode(svg,  90, 60, "Alice", {r: 28, stroke: "var(--cyan)", fontSize: 12});
    drawNode(svg, 630, 60, "Bob",   {r: 28, stroke: "var(--pink)", fontSize: 12});
    drawArrow(svg, [120, 60], [600, 60], {color: "var(--yellow)", label: `A = g^${a} = ${A}`, markerId: "arr-dh-A"});
    drawArrow(svg, [600, 100], [120, 100], {color: "var(--yellow)", label: `B = g^${b} = ${B}`, markerId: "arr-dh-B"});
    const t1 = SVG("text", {x: 90, y: 160, "text-anchor": "middle", "font-size": 12, fill: "var(--mute)"});
    t1.textContent = `K = B^a mod p = ${KAlice}`;
    svg.appendChild(t1);
    const t2 = SVG("text", {x: 630, y: 160, "text-anchor": "middle", "font-size": 12, fill: "var(--mute)"});
    t2.textContent = `K = A^b mod p = ${KBob}`;
    svg.appendChild(t2);
    const t3 = SVG("text", {x: 360, y: 200, "text-anchor": "middle", "font-size": 13, fill: "var(--green)"});
    t3.textContent = `shared K = g^(ab) mod ${p} = ${KAlice}`;
    svg.appendChild(t3);
  }
  ["#w-dh-p","#w-dh-g","#w-dh-a","#w-dh-b"].forEach(s => {
    $(s).addEventListener("input", go);
    $(s).addEventListener("change", go);
  });
  go();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Used in
Mathematics and cryptography · Elliptic-curve cryptography
Mathematics and cryptography · Lattice-based and post-quantum cryptography
Mathematics and cryptography · Zero-knowledge proofs

4. Elliptic-curve cryptography

Replace the multiplicative group $(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$ with the group of points $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ of an elliptic curve over a prime field. The chord-tangent construction equips $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ with an abelian group law, neutral element the point at infinity. Hasse's theorem bounds its order:

$$\#E(\mathbb{F}_p) = p + 1 - a_p, \qquad |a_p| \le 2\sqrt p.$$

The elliptic-curve discrete logarithm problem (ECDLP) asks, given $P, Q\in E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ with $Q = [k]P$, to recover $k$. On a generic curve no index-calculus attack is known — the only known classical attack is Pollard's $\rho$ in time $O(\sqrt{|E|})$. So 256-bit ECC delivers what 3072-bit RSA gives, and signed transactions on Bitcoin's secp256k1 stay short.

Pitfalls. Two attack families pull the ECDLP into easier groups:

  • MOV (Menezes–Okamoto–Vanstone). The Weil pairing embeds $E[\ell]\hookrightarrow \mathbb{F}_{p^k}^\times$ where $k$ is the embedding degree. Small $k$ ⇒ index calculus on $\mathbb{F}_{p^k}^\times$ wins.
  • Smart / anomalous curves. If $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p)=p$, the lift to $\mathbb{Z}_p$ linearises the DLP into addition.

Standard hardened curves (NIST P-256, Curve25519, secp256k1) avoid both. ECDH and ECDSA are the elliptic analogues of Diffie–Hellman and DSA — same protocol skeleton, different group.

W4 · Toy elliptic-curve point group$y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$
5
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const out = $("#w-ecc-out"), svg = $("#w-ecc-svg");
  const kVal = $("#w-ecc-k-val");
  function pointAdd(P, Q, a, p){
    if(P === null) return Q;
    if(Q === null) return P;
    const [x1,y1] = P, [x2,y2] = Q;
    if(x1 === x2 && (y1 + y2) % p === 0) return null;  // P + (-P) = O
    let m;
    if(x1 === x2 && y1 === y2){
      // doubling: m = (3 x1^2 + a) / (2 y1)
      const num = (3*x1*x1 + a) % p;
      const den = modInv((2*y1) % p, p);
      if(den === null) return null;
      m = ((num%p)+p)%p * den % p;
    } else {
      const num = ((y2 - y1) % p + p) % p;
      const den = modInv(((x2 - x1) % p + p) % p, p);
      if(den === null) return null;
      m = num * den % p;
    }
    const x3 = (m*m - x1 - x2) % p;
    const x3p = (x3 % p + p) % p;
    const y3 = (m * (x1 - x3p) - y1) % p;
    const y3p = (y3 % p + p) % p;
    return [x3p, y3p];
  }
  function go(){
    const p = parseInt($("#w-ecc-p").value, 10);
    const a = parseInt($("#w-ecc-a").value, 10);
    const b = parseInt($("#w-ecc-b").value, 10);
    const k = parseInt($("#w-ecc-k").value, 10);
    kVal.textContent = k;
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    if(!isPrimeSmall(p) || p < 5 || p > 97){
      out.textContent = "need a small prime 5 ≤ p ≤ 97";
      return;
    }
    // discriminant 4a^3 + 27b^2 != 0 mod p
    const disc = (4*a*a*a + 27*b*b) % p;
    if(((disc%p)+p)%p === 0){
      out.textContent = `singular curve: 4a³ + 27b² ≡ 0 (mod ${p})`;
      return;
    }
    // enumerate points
    const points = [];
    for(let x=0; x<p; x++){
      const rhs = ((x*x*x + a*x + b) % p + p) % p;
      for(let y=0; y<p; y++){
        if((y*y) % p === rhs) points.push([x,y]);
      }
    }
    // pick generator: first point
    const G = points[0] || null;
    let kG = null;
    if(G !== null){
      let acc = null, base = G;
      let kk = k;
      while(kk > 0){
        if(kk & 1) acc = pointAdd(acc, base, a, p);
        base = pointAdd(base, base, a, p);
        kk = Math.floor(kk/2);
      }
      kG = acc;
    }
    out.textContent =
      `E: y² = x³ + ${a}x + ${b}  over F_${p}\n` +
      `#E(F_${p}) = ${points.length + 1}  (Hasse: |#E - (p+1)| ≤ 2√p = ${(2*Math.sqrt(p)).toFixed(2)})\n` +
      (G ? `generator G = (${G[0]}, ${G[1]})\n` : "no affine points\n") +
      (kG === null ? `[${k}]G = O (point at infinity)` :
                     `[${k}]G = (${kG[0]}, ${kG[1]})`);
    // grid
    const W = 720, H = 380, pad = 30;
    const cw = (W - 2*pad) / p, ch = (H - 2*pad) / p;
    // grid background
    for(let i=0;i<=p;i++){
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line", {x1: pad + i*cw, y1: pad, x2: pad + i*cw, y2: H - pad,
        stroke: "var(--line)", "stroke-width": 0.5, opacity: 0.4}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line", {x1: pad, y1: pad + i*ch, x2: W - pad, y2: pad + i*ch,
        stroke: "var(--line)", "stroke-width": 0.5, opacity: 0.4}));
    }
    // points
    points.forEach(([x,y]) => {
      const cx = pad + x*cw + cw/2;
      const cy = H - pad - y*ch - ch/2;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx, cy, r: Math.min(4, cw/3), fill: "var(--blue)", opacity: 0.7}));
    });
    // highlight G
    if(G){
      const cx = pad + G[0]*cw + cw/2;
      const cy = H - pad - G[1]*ch - ch/2;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx, cy, r: Math.min(7, cw/2), fill: "none", stroke: "var(--yellow)", "stroke-width": 2}));
      const t = SVG("text", {x: cx + 10, y: cy - 8, "font-size": 11, fill: "var(--yellow)"});
      t.textContent = "G";
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // highlight kG
    if(kG){
      const cx = pad + kG[0]*cw + cw/2;
      const cy = H - pad - kG[1]*ch - ch/2;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx, cy, r: Math.min(7, cw/2), fill: "none", stroke: "var(--green)", "stroke-width": 2}));
      const t = SVG("text", {x: cx + 10, y: cy + 14, "font-size": 11, fill: "var(--green)"});
      t.textContent = `[${k}]G`;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
  }
  ["#w-ecc-p","#w-ecc-a","#w-ecc-b","#w-ecc-k"].forEach(s => {
    $(s).addEventListener("input", go);
    $(s).addEventListener("change", go);
  });
  go();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
5. Lattice-based and post-quantum cryptography

In 1994 Peter Shor exhibited polynomial-time quantum algorithms for integer factorization and discrete logarithms in any abelian group. A sufficiently large fault-tolerant quantum computer therefore breaks RSA, Diffie–Hellman, and ECC simultaneously. The hunt for replacement primitives — post-quantum cryptography — has settled, for now, on lattices.

A lattice $\Lambda \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is the integer span of $n$ linearly independent vectors $\mathbf{b}_1,\dots,\mathbf{b}_n$. Two notorious problems:

  • SVP — find a shortest nonzero vector of $\Lambda$.
  • CVP — given a target $\mathbf{t}\in\mathbb{R}^n$, find the lattice point closest to it.

Both are NP-hard in worst case (CVP exactly; SVP modulo a small approximation factor under randomized reductions). Even approximating them is hard for sub-polynomial factors.

Regev's Learning With Errors (LWE) (2005) bridges worst-case lattice hardness to an average-case key exchange. Pick a secret $\mathbf{s}\in\mathbb{Z}_q^n$. Samples are pairs $$(\mathbf{a}_i,\ \langle \mathbf{a}_i, \mathbf{s}\rangle + e_i \bmod q),$$ with $\mathbf{a}_i$ uniform and $e_i$ small Gaussian noise. Without the noise, Gaussian elimination recovers $\mathbf{s}$ from $n$ samples; with noise, the problem is conjectured (and quantum-reduces from worst-case approximate-SVP / GapSVP) to be hard.

CRYSTALS-Kyber — a Module-LWE KEM — became NIST FIPS 203 in 2024, with the related Dilithium signature scheme as FIPS 204. The construction reads as: generate noisy linear samples, encode a session key in the high bits of a lattice point, recover via the secret short vector.

W5 · LWE: noisy linear samplesadd noise — watch Gaussian elimination fail
2
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const out = $("#w-lwe-out"), svg = $("#w-lwe-svg"), nVal = $("#w-lwe-noise-val");
  function go(){
    const q = parseInt($("#w-lwe-q").value, 10);
    const s = parseInt($("#w-lwe-s").value, 10);
    const noise = parseInt($("#w-lwe-noise").value, 10);
    nVal.textContent = noise;
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    if(!Number.isFinite(q) || q < 5 || s < 0 || s >= q){
      out.textContent = `need q ≥ 5 and 0 ≤ s < q`;
      return;
    }
    const samples = [];
    const N = 12;
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++){
      const a = Math.floor(Math.random()*q);
      const e = Math.floor(Math.random()*(2*noise+1)) - noise;
      const b = ((a*s + e) % q + q) % q;
      samples.push({a,b,e});
    }
    out.textContent =
      `q = ${q},  secret s = ${s},  noise bound = ${noise}\n` +
      `samples (a,  a*s + e mod q):\n` +
      samples.map(({a,b,e}) => `  a=${String(a).padStart(2)}    b=${String(b).padStart(2)}    (e=${e>=0?"+":""}${e})`).join("\n") +
      `\n\nNoise=0 ⇒ b/a recovers s exactly (when gcd(a,q)=1).\nNoise>0 ⇒ every sample looks uniformly random; recovery is LWE-hard.`;
    // scatter plot of (a, b)
    const W = 720, H = 220, pad = 28;
    const x = a => pad + a/q * (W - 2*pad);
    const y = b => H - pad - b/q * (H - 2*pad - 30);
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line", {x1: pad, y1: H-pad, x2: W-pad, y2: H-pad, stroke: "var(--line)"}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line", {x1: pad, y1: pad, x2: pad, y2: H-pad, stroke: "var(--line)"}));
    // ideal line b = a*s mod q (drawn as wrapped points)
    for(let a=0;a<q;a++){
      const bIdeal = (a*s) % q;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx: x(a), cy: y(bIdeal), r: 2, fill: "var(--mute)", opacity: 0.6}));
    }
    // samples
    samples.forEach(({a,b}) => {
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle", {cx: x(a), cy: y(b), r: 5, fill: "var(--yellow)", opacity: 0.85}));
    });
    const t1 = SVG("text", {x: pad, y: 16, "font-size": 11, fill: "var(--mute)"});
    t1.textContent = `gray = ideal lattice line b=a·s; yellow = noisy samples`;
    svg.appendChild(t1);
    const t2 = SVG("text", {x: W-pad, y: H-10, "font-size": 11, fill: "var(--mute)", "text-anchor": "end"});
    t2.textContent = `axes: a horizontally, b = a*s + e mod q vertically`;
    svg.appendChild(t2);
  }
  $("#w-lwe-go").addEventListener("click", go);
  ["#w-lwe-q","#w-lwe-s","#w-lwe-noise"].forEach(s => {
    $(s).addEventListener("input", go);
    $(s).addEventListener("change", go);
  });
  go();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
6. Zero-knowledge proofs

Goldwasser, Micali, and Rackoff (1985) introduced interactive proofs: a randomized protocol between a prover $P$ and a verifier $V$, with three properties for a language $L$:

  • Completeness. $x \in L$ ⇒ honest $P$ convinces $V$ with probability $\ge 2/3$.
  • Soundness. $x \notin L$ ⇒ no cheating prover $P^*$ convinces $V$ with probability $> 1/3$.
  • Zero-knowledge. The verifier's transcript can be simulated from $x$ alone — $V$ learns only the truth of the statement.

The canonical example is Schnorr's protocol (1989) for proving knowledge of a discrete log $x$ with $h = g^x$:

  1. Prover picks random $r$, sends commitment $t = g^r$.
  2. Verifier sends random challenge $c$.
  3. Prover sends response $s = r + cx$.
  4. Verifier accepts iff $g^s = t \cdot h^c$.

Soundness: a prover who can answer two distinct challenges $c \ne c'$ for the same $t$ leaks $x = (s - s') / (c - c')$. Zero-knowledge: a simulator chooses $s$ and $c$ first, then sets $t = g^s / h^c$ — the resulting transcript is indistinguishable from a real one.

Modern zk-SNARKs (Groth16 (2016), PLONK (2019), STARKs) compile any NP statement into a few-kilobyte non-interactive proof verifiable in milliseconds. They are the engine of Zcash's shielded transactions, Ethereum zk-rollups, and verifiable computation services. The Fiat–Shamir transform turns any honest-verifier interactive ZK protocol into a non-interactive one by replacing the verifier's random challenge with a hash of the transcript so far — a structural bridge from Schnorr to SNARK.
W6 · Schnorr protocol — honest runadvance the protocol step by step
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const out = $("#w-sch-out"), svg = $("#w-sch-svg");
  let step = 0, state = null;
  function reset(){
    step = 0;
    state = null;
    render();
  }
  function advance(){
    const p = parseInt($("#w-sch-p").value, 10);
    const g = parseInt($("#w-sch-g").value, 10);
    const x = parseInt($("#w-sch-x").value, 10);
    if(!isPrimeSmall(p) || g < 2 || g >= p || x < 1 || x >= p){
      out.textContent = "need prime p, 2 ≤ g < p, 1 ≤ x < p";
      return;
    }
    if(step === 0){
      // commit
      const r = 1 + Math.floor(Math.random() * (p - 2));
      const t = modPow(g, r, p);
      const h = modPow(g, x, p);
      state = {p, g, x, r, t, h, c: null, s: null};
      step = 1;
    } else if(step === 1){
      // challenge
      state.c = 1 + Math.floor(Math.random() * (p - 2));
      step = 2;
    } else if(step === 2){
      // response
      const order = p - 1;
      state.s = ((state.r + state.c * state.x) % order + order) % order;
      step = 3;
    } else {
      reset();
      return;
    }
    render();
  }
  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML = "";
    if(!state){
      out.textContent = "Click \"step\" to begin Schnorr's protocol.";
      drawNode(svg,  90, 80, "Prover",   {r: 32, stroke: "var(--cyan)", fontSize: 11});
      drawNode(svg, 630, 80, "Verifier", {r: 32, stroke: "var(--pink)", fontSize: 11});
      return;
    }
    const {p, g, x, r, t, h, c, s} = state;
    let report =
      `public:  p = ${p},  g = ${g},  h = g^x mod p = ${h}    (x is secret)\n`;
    if(step >= 1) report += `» step 1 (commit):  prover picks r=${r}, sends t = g^r mod p = ${t}\n`;
    if(step >= 2) report += `» step 2 (challenge): verifier sends random c = ${c}\n`;
    if(step >= 3){
      const lhs = modPow(g, s, p);
      const rhs = (t * modPow(h, c, p)) % p;
      report += `» step 3 (response):  prover sends s = r + c*x mod (p-1) = ${s}\n`;
      report += `» verify:  g^s mod p = ${lhs}    t * h^c mod p = ${rhs}    ${(lhs===rhs)?"✓ accept":"✗ reject"}\n`;
      report += `(zero-knowledge: x never appears in the transcript)`;
    }
    out.textContent = report;
    // diagram
    drawNode(svg,  90, 60, "Prover",   {r: 32, stroke: "var(--cyan)", fontSize: 11});
    drawNode(svg, 630, 60, "Verifier", {r: 32, stroke: "var(--pink)", fontSize: 11});
    if(step >= 1) drawArrow(svg, [122, 60], [598, 60], {color: "var(--yellow)", label: `t = ${t}`, markerId: "arr-sch-t"});
    if(step >= 2) drawArrow(svg, [598, 110], [122, 110], {color: "var(--violet)", label: `c = ${c}`, markerId: "arr-sch-c"});
    if(step >= 3) drawArrow(svg, [122, 160], [598, 160], {color: "var(--green)", label: `s = ${s}`, markerId: "arr-sch-s"});
  }
  $("#w-sch-step").addEventListener("click", advance);
  $("#w-sch-reset").addEventListener("click", reset);
  ["#w-sch-p","#w-sch-g","#w-sch-x"].forEach(s => $(s).addEventListener("change", reset));
  reset();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Connections

Cryptography is one of the few places where unproved hardness conjectures cash out as deployed engineering. The settled-looking landscape — RSA, ECC, lattices — sits on a small pile of conjectured separations: factoring is conjecturally not in $\mathsf{P}$, the discrete log is conjecturally hard on $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$, average-case LWE conjecturally reduces from worst-case lattice problems. Open frontiers: a fault-tolerant quantum computer large enough to run Shor end-to-end on $2048$-bit RSA, post-quantum signature standardisation beyond Dilithium, succinct proofs without trusted setup, and obfuscation built on plausible primitives.

  • Abstract algebra — groups, rings, fields — the unit group $(\mathbb{Z}/n)^\times$ and Lagrange's theorem are the foundation under Fermat–Euler.
  • Elliptic curves — the chord-tangent group law is the algebraic substrate of ECDH and ECDSA.
  • Complexity theory — P vs NP — every cryptographic hardness assumption is a refusal to believe a polynomial-time algorithm exists.
  • NP-completeness — lattice problems join the hardness web through Karp reductions; the Cook–Levin paradigm underwrites SNARK soundness via reductions to circuit-SAT.
  • p-adics — local-global — the Hasse principle organises the same "study a Diophantine problem prime-by-prime" mindset that index-calculus attacks exploit.
